items.question_text: When may a controller enlist the services of military radar facilities and other emergency resources to assist an aircraft in distress?
items.explanation: Per paragraph 10-1-3, controllers provide maximum assistance and may enlist available radar facilities and emergency services when the pilot requests OR when the controller deems necessary. The controller has independent authority to enlist resources.

choices.text: Only after the pilot specifically requests the additional resource by name.
When the pilot requests assistance or when the controller deems it necessary.
Only when the FAA facility becomes inoperable and can no longer provide any radar service.
Only after obtaining written authorization from the facility air traffic manager.

items.question_text: What obligation does a controller have upon learning that a VFR aircraft without a flight plan is overdue?
items.explanation: Per paragraph 10-1-5, controllers must coordinate efforts to the extent possible to assist any aircraft believed overdue, lost, or in emergency status. The obligation applies regardless of flight rules or whether a flight plan was filed.

choices.text: Wait for the aircraft operator to file a missing aircraft report before initiating any action.
Coordinate efforts to the extent possible, because the obligation applies regardless of flight plan status.
Notify only the local FSS and take no further action for VFR aircraft without flight plans.
No obligation exists because VFR aircraft operating without flight plans are outside ATC jurisdiction.

items.question_text: When emergency equipment from outside the airport proper must cross active movement areas to reach an accident scene, what is the controller's traffic management obligation?
items.explanation: Per paragraph 10-1-6a, the traffic-control obligation applies when routes within the airport proper are required for movement of local emergency equipment going to or from an emergency, even when the emergency occurs outside the airport proper. The controller must control other traffic to avoid conflicts along those routes.

choices.text: Close the airport until emergency vehicles confirm they have reached their destination.
Control other air and ground traffic to avoid conflicts along the routes the emergency vehicles need to use.
No obligation exists because off-airport emergency vehicles are not FAA-operated equipment.
Provide traffic advisories only — emergency vehicles are responsible for their own separation on the movement area.

items.question_text: What is the minimum required information that must be obtained before beginning assistance to an aircraft in an inflight emergency?
items.explanation: Per paragraph 10-2-1a, minimum required information for inflight emergencies is: aircraft identification and type, nature of the emergency, and pilot's desires. Assistance must start as soon as enough information has been obtained upon which to act.

choices.text: Aircraft identification, fuel remaining in time, and number of people on board.
Aircraft identification and type, nature of the emergency, and pilot's desires.
Nature of the emergency, pilot's qualifications, and aircraft altitude.
All 17 items listed in the paragraph including both minimum and supplementary information.

items.question_text: A military fighter-type aircraft at low altitude declares an emergency and the controller has the minimum required information. The controller wants to ask the pilot several items from the supplementary list. What does the guidance say about requesting supplementary information from low-altitude military fighters?
items.explanation: Per Note 3 to paragraph 10-2-1b, controllers should normally not request supplementary information from military fighter-type aircraft at low altitudes. The single exception is: request the position of an aircraft that is not visually sighted or displayed on radar if the location is not given by the pilot.

choices.text: There is no exception — the prohibition on requesting supplementary information from low-altitude fighters is absolute.
The controller should request fuel remaining, since fuel status is always critical regardless of aircraft type.
The controller should request the aircraft's position if it is not visually sighted, on radar, or given by the pilot.
The controller should request all 14 supplementary items because the emergency makes every piece of information critical.

items.question_text: What hazard does the note to the altitude-change guidance identify for aircraft with high-bypass turbofan engines that have entered a volcanic ash cloud?
items.explanation: Per the Note to paragraph 10-2-4, damage to engines due to volcanic ash ingestion increases as engine power is increased. Therefore, climb while in the ash cloud is to be avoided where terrain permits. High-bypass turbofan engines (such as B747) are especially vulnerable to this effect.

choices.text: Ash particles clog pitot-static systems at higher altitudes, causing unreliable airspeed and altitude indications.
Engine damage from ash ingestion increases as engine power increases, so climb in the ash cloud is avoided where terrain permits.
The ash cloud's electrical properties cause complete communications blackout above a certain altitude threshold.
Engine thrust actually improves in ash because the particles act as an abrasive that cleans turbine blades.

items.question_text: What is required before an unexpected loss of radar contact combined with loss of radio communications triggers the obligation to consider an aircraft emergency exists?
items.explanation: Per paragraph 10-2-5b, the trigger is unexpected loss of BOTH radar contact AND radio communications with any IFR or VFR aircraft. The conjunction 'and' means both conditions must be present. Loss of only one does not satisfy the trigger.

choices.text: Loss of radar contact alone is sufficient, because radar is the primary means of aircraft surveillance.
Both radar contact and radio communications must be unexpectedly lost — loss of only one does not trigger the requirement.
Loss of radio communications alone is sufficient, because voice contact is essential for emergency coordination.
Either loss of radar contact or loss of radio communications independently triggers the requirement.

items.question_text: What steps must a controller complete when a VFR aircraft in weather difficulty requires a frequency change to another facility for better service?
items.explanation: Per paragraph 10-2-7a, the controller must advise the pilot of the reason for the change, request the aircraft contact the appropriate facility, and inform that facility of the situation. If the aircraft cannot communicate with the new facility, the original controller must relay information and clearances.

choices.text: Issue the new frequency without explanation to keep transmissions brief during the weather emergency.
Advise the pilot of the reason for the change, request contact with the new facility, and inform that facility of the situation.
Hand off using standard radar procedures — no special coordination is required before an emergency is formally declared.
Transfer communications only after the receiving facility confirms it has established radar contact with the aircraft.

items.question_text: What condition permits a controller to provide radar assistance to a VFR pilot who lacks IFR qualifications, has an unequipped aircraft, and declines to declare an emergency?
items.explanation: Per paragraph 10-2-8c, radar assistance may be provided when the pilot refuses to declare an emergency but the controller independently determines that an emergency condition exists or is imminent. The controller's determination under 10-1-1c operates independently of the pilot's declaration.

choices.text: The controller must first obtain supervisory approval before providing any radar assistance without a pilot declaration.
Radar assistance is automatic for any aircraft in IMC regardless of pilot qualifications or declarations.
When the controller determines an emergency condition exists or is imminent, even if the pilot refuses to declare one.
Radar assistance may never be provided to a pilot who is not IFR qualified and refuses to declare an emergency.

items.question_text: When providing radar assistance to a pilot who lacks IFR qualifications, when should turns be executed and which maneuver should be deferred while the aircraft is in IFR conditions?
items.explanation: Per paragraph 10-2-9b and 10-2-9d, turns should be made while the aircraft is in VFR conditions so it can fly straight in IFR conditions. Controllers should avoid requiring a climb or descent while the aircraft is in a turn and in IFR conditions, as this increases spatial disorientation risk.

choices.text: Make all turns in IFR conditions so the pilot can practice instrument scanning during the emergency.
Make turns in VFR conditions; avoid requiring climb or descent while turning in IFR conditions.
Combine turns with altitude changes to minimize total time spent in instrument meteorological conditions.
Make turns only after the aircraft has descended to the MVA, regardless of whether in VFR or IFR conditions.

items.question_text: What information must a controller obtain when a pilot facing a bomb threat requests or appears to need technical assistance, and what must the controller refrain from doing?
items.explanation: Per paragraph 10-2-11a6, the controller must obtain the aircraft type/series/model, precise location/description of the device if known, and other pertinent details, then notify the supervisor who will contact the FAA aviation explosives expert. The controller must refrain from suggesting what actions the pilot should take concerning the bomb.

choices.text: Suggest that the pilot land immediately and evacuate; then notify the supervisor of the pilot's intended actions.
Obtain aircraft type, device location and description, and other pertinent details; notify the supervisor; refrain from suggesting pilot actions.
Ask the pilot to describe the device in detail so the controller can provide step-by-step bomb-handling instructions.
Transfer the aircraft to the supervisor's frequency so the supervisor can directly advise the pilot on security procedures.

items.question_text: What are the three sequential steps a controller takes when an aircraft requests the location of the nearest explosive detection K-9 team?
items.explanation: Per paragraph 10-2-12, the three-step process is: (1) obtain aircraft identification and position, advise supervisor; (2) when the nearest K-9 location is received, relay it to the pilot; (3) if the aircraft wishes to divert to that location, obtain an ETA and advise the supervisor.

choices.text: Contact the K-9 team directly; relay the aircraft's position to the team; issue a landing clearance to the team's airport.
Obtain aircraft ID and position, advise supervisor; relay the K-9 location to the pilot; if diverting, obtain ETA and advise supervisor.
Advise the supervisor; wait for the supervisor to coordinate with the K-9 team; relay the supervisor's instructions to the pilot.
Issue an immediate clearance to the nearest suitable airport; coordinate K-9 availability after the aircraft has landed.

items.question_text: What seven factors must a controller consider when recommending an emergency airport?
items.explanation: Per paragraph 10-2-15a, the seven factors are: remaining fuel in relation to airport distances, weather conditions, airport conditions, NAVAID status, aircraft type, pilot's qualifications, and vectoring or homing capability to the emergency airport. The note indicates weather may deserve weighted consideration.

choices.text: Fuel remaining, weather, runway length, firefighting capability, aircraft type, and pilot's total flight hours.
Fuel remaining, weather, airport conditions, NAVAID status, aircraft type, pilot qualifications, and vectoring or homing capability.
Distance to airport, fuel remaining, weather, aircraft weight, number of passengers, and emergency equipment availability.
NAVAID status, weather, airport elevation, runway surface type, aircraft category, and pilot's medical certificate class.

items.question_text: For a turbine-powered aircraft that has entered a volcanic ash cloud and indicated a distress situation exists, when may a climb clearance be initiated by ATC?
items.explanation: Per paragraph 10-2-18b, ATC should not initiate a climb clearance unless the pilot requests it or ATC first obtains the pilot's concurrence. This is because climb while in the ash cloud increases engine damage from ash ingestion (per Note to 10-2-4), especially for high-bypass turbofan engines.

choices.text: Immediately, because exiting the ash cloud is the highest priority regardless of engine damage risk.
Only when the pilot requests a climb or after ATC obtains the pilot's concurrence.
Only after the aircraft has exited the ash cloud and all engines have been verified to be operating normally.
Climb is prohibited for all turbine-powered aircraft in ash clouds — descent is the only authorized vertical maneuver.

items.question_text: What information must a controller obtain upon receiving a report of a death, illness, or other public health risk on board an aircraft?
items.explanation: Per paragraph 10-2-19a, the controller must obtain: call sign, number of suspected cases of illness, nature of the illnesses or other public health risk (if known), number of persons on board, number of deaths (if any), and departure and destination airports. This information is then relayed to the OMIC/OS/CIC.

choices.text: Call sign, aircraft type, pilot's name, number of persons on board, and estimated time of arrival.
Call sign, number of suspected illness cases, nature of illness if known, number of persons on board, number of deaths if any, and departure and destination airports.
Call sign, nature of illness, fuel remaining, aircraft color, and number of deaths.
Call sign, departure and destination airports, number of persons on board, and pilot's intentions for diversion.

items.question_text: After what three trigger events, combined with 30 minutes without communications or radar contact, is an aircraft considered overdue for ALNOT purposes?
items.explanation: Per paragraph 10-3-1a, an aircraft is considered overdue when 30 minutes have passed with neither communications nor radar contact since: (1) its ETA over a specified or compulsory reporting point or at a clearance limit, (2) its clearance void time, or (3) failure of a VFR or IFR aircraft to cancel its flight plan within 30 minutes after destination ETA. The procedures also apply to 'missing' or 'unreported' aircraft.

choices.text: The time the flight plan was filed, the time of last radar contact, or the time the pilot last requested a weather briefing.
ETA over a reporting point or clearance limit, clearance void time, or failure to cancel a flight plan within 30 minutes of destination ETA.
The time the aircraft departed, the time of last fuel stop, or the time the flight plan was activated.
The time of last position report, the time of last altitude change, or the time the aircraft entered the controller's airspace.

items.question_text: When comparing the information forwarded by a terminal facility to the ARTCC with the information the ARTCC later forwards to the RCC, which items are unique to the RCC notification?
items.explanation: Per paragraphs 10-3-2 and 10-3-3, the ARTCC adds two items when forwarding to the RCC that the terminal facility does not send to the ARTCC: 'facility and person calling' and 'action taken by reporting facility and proposed action.' All other items — flight plan, last transmission, last position, beacon code, persons on board, fuel status, ELT information, and remarks — are common to both notification lists.

choices.text: The notification lists are identical — the ARTCC simply forwards the same information the terminal facility provided.
Facility and person calling, and action taken by reporting facility and proposed action.
Aircraft beacon code and number of persons on board — these are terminal-only items withheld from the RCC.
Fuel status and ELT signal information — these are added by the ARTCC when the terminal facility omits them.

items.question_text: What is the normal geographic scope of an ALNOT, and under what circumstances may it be expanded?
items.explanation: Per paragraph 10-3-4a, the ALNOT is normally issued to all centers and Area B circuits covering 50 miles on either side of the route from the last reported position to destination. At the recommendation of the RCC or at the controller's discretion, the ALNOT may be expanded to cover the maximum range of the aircraft from the last known position.

choices.text: 100 miles either side of the route — the 50-mile figure in the paragraph refers to nautical miles from each reporting point.
50 miles either side of the route from last known position to destination; expandable at RCC recommendation or controller discretion.
The entire ARTCC airspace — ALNOTs automatically cover all sectors within the issuing center's boundary.
50 miles either side, and this scope is mandatory — there is no authority to expand the distribution area.

items.question_text: What is the two-tier procedure for providing an overdue aircraft's last known position to the RCC when the formal extraction tool will cause delay?
items.explanation: Per paragraph 10-3-6b, if necessary to prevent undue delay, utilize any available method to determine an initial latitude and longitude, then follow up as soon as possible with a formal lat/long using the appropriate data extraction tools. This balances SAR urgency with accuracy.

choices.text: Wait for the formal extraction — only the most accurate position data meets the standard for SAR coordination.
Provide an initial position using any available method immediately, then follow up with the formal lat/long as soon as possible.
Advise the RCC that position data is unavailable and request they proceed using the aircraft's last reported waypoint.
Use only the formal extraction tool but request the RCC expedite planning using a rough estimate in the interim.

items.question_text: Who directs the cancellation of an ALNOT, and what information must the cancellation include?
items.explanation: Per paragraph 10-3-7, the ALNOT is canceled when directed by the RCC after the aircraft is located or the search is abandoned. The cancellation must include pertinent information to aid the RCC, SAR teams, and FAA SAR management, explicitly including the location where the aircraft or wreckage was found.

choices.text: The issuing ARTCC cancels the ALNOT autonomously once the aircraft is located; no additional information is required.
The RCC directs cancellation; the cancellation includes the location of the aircraft or wreckage and other pertinent information.
The ALNOT expires automatically 24 hours after issuance; no formal cancellation message is needed.
The terminal facility that originated the overdue report directs cancellation after confirming the aircraft has landed safely.

items.question_text: What five time references, in order of priority, determine when the 30-minute IFR traffic restriction period begins for an overdue or unreported aircraft?
items.explanation: Per paragraph 10-4-1, the five time references in priority order are: (a) approach clearance delivery time, (b) EFC time, (c) arrival time over the NAVAID serving the destination, (d) the controller's current estimate, and (e) the time the flight plan was filed. When a higher-priority reference is available, it takes precedence.

choices.text: EFC time, approach clearance time, flight plan filing time, controller's estimate, and NAVAID estimate.
Approach clearance time, EFC time, NAVAID arrival estimate, controller's current estimate, and flight plan filing time as a last resort.
All five times are used simultaneously, and the restriction clock runs from the earliest of the five.
Only the controller's current estimate and the flight plan filing time — the other references are advisory only.

items.question_text: For what duration must airport lighting systems be operated for an unreported aircraft, and how does the responsibility differ between en route and terminal facilities?
items.explanation: Per paragraph 10-4-2, lighting must be operated for at least 30 minutes before the ETA until the aircraft is located or for 30 minutes after fuel exhaustion. En route facilities at nontower/non-FSS locations request airport management to activate the lights. Terminal facilities operate the lights directly.

choices.text: Exactly 30 minutes before ETA until exactly 30 minutes after ETA, regardless of whether the aircraft is located sooner.
At least 30 minutes before ETA until located or 30 minutes after fuel exhaustion; en route requests airport management, terminal operates directly.
Only the runway edge lights are required — approach lights and other systems are activated at the controller's discretion.
Lighting is a pilot responsibility at nontower airports; ATC has no obligation to activate or request airport lighting.

items.question_text: After the 30-minute traffic suspension period for an overdue aircraft expires, what are the requirements for resuming normal ATC?
items.explanation: Per paragraph 10-4-3, normal ATC may resume if the operators or pilots of other affected aircraft concur, and this concurrence must be maintained for 30 minutes after the suspension period expires. If concurrence is withdrawn during that 30-minute window, normal ATC must be suspended again. Concurrence is required from all affected operators/pilots.

choices.text: Normal ATC resumes automatically once the suspension period expires — no concurrence or further action is required.
Affected operators or pilots must concur, and that concurrence must be maintained for 30 minutes after the suspension period expires.
The facility air traffic manager must authorize resumption in writing after verifying the suspension period has fully elapsed.
A simple majority of affected pilots must concur — unanimous concurrence from all affected operators is not required.

items.question_text: Five minutes have elapsed since two-way radio communications with an IFR aircraft were lost. What additional action is required?
items.explanation: After 5 minutes without reestablished communications, consider the aircraft's or pilot's activity suspicious and report it to the OS/CIC under the suspicious-flight procedures. Attempts to reestablish communications and other appropriate lost-communications actions continue; the five-minute mark adds the suspicious-activity reporting requirement.

choices.text: Continue ordinary communications attempts; no additional notification is required until the aircraft becomes overdue.
Consider the aircraft's or pilot's activity suspicious and report it to the OS/CIC while continuing appropriate lost-communications actions.
Immediately issue an ALNOT and notify the RCC, regardless of the aircraft's route or other available information.
Terminate further communication attempts and protect only the aircraft's filed route.

items.question_text: When an aircraft carrying explosive cargo is making an emergency landing, to which entities must the explosive cargo information be relayed?
items.explanation: Per paragraph 10-5-1, the explosive cargo information must be relayed to the emergency equipment crew, the airport management, and — when requested by the pilot — the appropriate military agencies. The controller must also inform the pilot of the safest or least congested airport areas.

choices.text: Only the emergency equipment crew, because they are the primary first responders to any aircraft emergency.
The emergency equipment crew, airport management, and — when the pilot requests — the appropriate military agencies.
The airport management only, who then assumes responsibility for notifying all other relevant entities.
The emergency equipment crew, airport management, and military agencies — military notification is mandatory regardless of pilot request.

items.question_text: What actions must a controller take for an airborne aircraft already within an activated Debris Response Area (DRA) following a space launch mishap?
items.explanation: Per paragraph 10-5-2, for aircraft already within an activated DRA, the controller must issue the DRA location, provide hazard information, and advise the pilot to exit the DRA. The alert broadcast must be reissued every 5 minutes for 20 minutes following the last known event.

choices.text: Clear the aircraft to land at the nearest airport immediately, regardless of position relative to the debris field.
Issue the DRA location, provide hazard information, and advise the pilot to exit the DRA.
Maintain normal ATC services — once inside the DRA, changing course could increase collision risk.
Issue only the DRA location and let the pilot determine whether to remain in or exit the area.

items.question_text: An oceanic aircraft's expected communication time has passed and 35 minutes have elapsed without contact. Which emergency phase applies?
items.explanation: Per paragraph 10-6-2a, the Uncertainty Phase (INCERFA) exists when communication has not been received within 30 minutes after the time a communication should have been received, or after the first unsuccessful attempt to establish communication, whichever is earlier. This is the first and least severe of the three oceanic emergency phases.

choices.text: Alert Phase (ALERFA), because any communication failure beyond 30 minutes escalates to the alert level.
Uncertainty Phase (INCERFA).
Distress Phase (DETRESFA), because loss of communication in oceanic airspace always indicates grave danger.
No emergency phase exists until the aircraft also fails to arrive at its destination.

items.question_text: When an aircraft over water requests sea condition information or surface vessel assistance, from what source is this information obtained?
items.explanation: Per paragraph 10-6-4a, weather, sea conditions, ditching information, and surface vessel assistance should be requested from the RCC. An AMVER SURPIC should also be requested if the aircraft asks or if control personnel deem it beneficial.

choices.text: From the aircraft's company dispatch, because the operator arranges maritime assistance for its own flights.
From the nearest Coast Guard station, because maritime rescue coordination is a Coast Guard responsibility.
From the RCC, with an AMVER SURPIC requested if the aircraft asks or the controller deems it beneficial.
From the AMVER Center only, because the automated system provides all necessary weather and sea condition data.

items.question_text: What type of clearance limit should be used for a SAR aircraft being positioned to assist a distressed aircraft in oceanic airspace?
items.explanation: Per paragraph 10-6-5b, the SAR aircraft must be cleared to a fixed clearance limit rather than to the aircraft in distress, which is a moving fix. Route clearances should be consistent with that of the distressed aircraft. A moving fix creates an unstable clearance boundary.

choices.text: The distressed aircraft's current position, to minimize the time needed to establish visual contact.
A fixed clearance limit, because the distressed aircraft is a moving fix that cannot serve as a stable clearance boundary.
The nearest suitable airport, since all SAR operations in oceanic airspace must terminate at a landing facility.
No clearance limit — SAR aircraft operating under emergency authority receive only heading and altitude assignments.

items.question_text: What entities must issue alerting advisories for a ground missile emergency in the en route environment?
items.explanation: Per paragraph 10-7-1, en route facilities must have alerting advisories issued by air carrier company radio stations (for VFR company aircraft in the vicinity) and by FSSs adjacent to the emergency location. Terminal facilities relay all information to the ARTCC and disseminate locally.

choices.text: Only the ARTCC, which assumes all alerting responsibility for ground missile emergencies regardless of facility type.
Air carrier company radio stations for VFR company aircraft and FSSs adjacent to the emergency location.
The nearest approach control facility and the overlying ARTCC, operating jointly.
Only air carrier company radio stations — FSS participation is limited to terminal-area emergencies.

items.question_text: What lateral and vertical separation minima apply when rerouting IFR and SVFR aircraft around a ground missile emergency location?
items.explanation: Per paragraph 10-7-2, the minima are: 1 mile lateral separation between the emergency location and the aircraft under radar control (or the protected route airspace), and 6,000 feet vertical separation above the surface over the emergency location. Greater minima apply when suggested by the notifying official.

choices.text: 3 miles lateral and 2,000 feet vertical — the standard IFR separation minima applied to all ground hazards.
1 mile lateral and 6,000 feet vertical, or greater when suggested by the notifying official.
5 miles lateral and 4,000 feet vertical, which is the standard en route emergency separation minimum.
2 miles lateral and 3,000 feet vertical, which is the reduced separation standard authorized for emergency operations.

items.question_text: What must controllers advise all aircraft to do regarding smoke columns in the vicinity of a ground missile emergency?
items.explanation: Per paragraph 10-7-4, controllers must advise all aircraft to avoid any observed smoke columns in the vicinity of a ground missile emergency. This applies to all aircraft regardless of flight rules or the type of ATC service being received.

choices.text: Fly above the smoke column, since vertical separation is the approved avoidance method.
Avoid any observed smoke columns.
Report the smoke column's position and estimated height for inclusion in the next ATIS broadcast.
No specific smoke advisory is required — the standard separation minima cover all associated hazards.

items.instruction: Choose the action that respects the pilot in command's emergency decision.
items.question_text: A controller recommends an alternative diversion airport to a distressed pilot, but the pilot insists on the original destination. What is the correct controller action?

choices.text: Continue assisting toward the original destination — the controller recommends, the pilot decides.
Override the pilot and issue an amended clearance to the alternative airport the controller recommended.
Withhold further vectors until the pilot acknowledges the safety advantages of the recommended airport.
Transfer the aircraft to a supervisor to resolve the destination disagreement.

items.explanation: Paragraph 10-1-2a requires assistance consistent with the pilot's requests. The controller may recommend alternatives but must not override the pilot's decision. 14 CFR § 91.3 reinforces pilot-in-command authority.

items.instruction: Select the correct combination of controller actions for a military fighter-type aircraft emergency at low altitude.
items.question_text: Which combination of controller actions correctly follows the guidance for an inflight emergency involving a military fighter-type aircraft at low altitude?

choices.text: Keep the current frequency and transponder code, and transmit only essential communications.
Switch to 121.5 MHz, assign a discrete emergency squawk, and request a full aircraft status report.
Change to a clear frequency, assign a new transponder code for better tracking, and maintain normal communication flow.
Transfer communications to the military base frequency and instruct the pilot to report all system statuses.

items.explanation: Paragraph 10-1-7a states that frequency and transponder code changes should be avoided and radio transmissions held to a minimum, especially at low altitude. The pilot's full attention is required to maintain control of the aircraft.

items.instruction: Identify the correct sequence for gathering and acting on emergency information.
items.question_text: Which sequence correctly describes how a controller should gather information and begin assisting an inflight emergency?

choices.text: Obtain the three minimum items, begin assistance, then gather supplementary items as necessary.
Gather all 17 information items, then begin assistance once the complete operational picture is known.
Begin assistance immediately upon hearing any distress call, without waiting for any specific information items.
Gather the 14 supplementary items first, then ask for aircraft identification and emergency nature last.

items.explanation: Paragraph 10-2-1a requires starting assistance as soon as the three minimum items are obtained. The 14 supplementary items in paragraph 10-2-1b are obtained after initiating action, as necessary.

items.instruction: Identify which event triggers the requirement to consider an aircraft emergency exists.
items.question_text: Which situation requires a controller to consider that an aircraft emergency exists and inform the RCC or ARTCC?

choices.text: An emergency is declared by a system-generated transmission from an aircraft.
An aircraft makes a routine position report two minutes later than the expected reporting time.
A pilot reports a minor avionics cooling fan failure that has no effect on flight safety.
An aircraft requests a deviation around a small area of light precipitation while remaining in VMC.

items.explanation: Paragraph 10-2-5a lists specific emergency declaration sources including system-generated transmissions. Routine operational events, minor equipment issues without safety impact, and standard weather deviations do not trigger the emergency consideration requirement.

items.instruction: Select the condition that allows radar assistance to a VFR pilot who is not IFR qualified and refuses to declare an emergency.
items.question_text: A VFR pilot in IMC is not IFR qualified and refuses to declare an emergency. Under which condition may the controller provide radar assistance?

choices.text: The controller determines an emergency condition exists or is imminent, regardless of the pilot's refusal to declare one.
Radar assistance is automatic for any aircraft that enters IMC, regardless of pilot qualifications or declarations.
Radar assistance may be provided only after the pilot files an IFR flight plan with the appropriate controlling agency.
Radar assistance is prohibited unless the pilot explicitly declares an emergency or holds a valid instrument rating.

items.explanation: Paragraph 10-2-8c allows radar assistance when the pilot refuses to declare an emergency but the controller independently determines emergency conditions exist or are imminent. The controller's determination under 10-1-1c operates independently of the pilot's declaration.

items.instruction: Choose the correct radar-assistance technique for a non-IFR-qualified pilot.
items.question_text: Which technique should a controller apply when providing radar assistance to a pilot not qualified for IFR operations?

choices.text: Make turns while the aircraft is in VFR conditions so it can fly a straight course while in IFR conditions.
Require a descent while the aircraft is in a turn, because combining maneuvers reduces total time spent in IMC.
Change frequencies frequently to identify the clearest communications channel available.
Have the pilot lower the landing gear after entering IMC, since configuration changes are less disorienting in clouds.

items.explanation: Paragraph 10-2-9b instructs controllers to make turns while the aircraft is in VFR conditions. Paragraph 10-2-9d states that climb or descent should be avoided while turning in IFR conditions. Paragraph 10-2-9a advises against frequency changes except when necessary. Paragraph 10-2-9c says to configure the aircraft while in VFR conditions.

items.instruction: Identify the correct ELT notification responsibility.
items.question_text: How does the ELT signal notification responsibility differ between en route and terminal facilities?

choices.text: En route notifies the RCC directly; terminal notifies the ARTCC, which coordinates with the RCC.
Both facility types notify the RCC directly, and terminal facilities make an additional courtesy call to the ARTCC.
En route notifies the ARTCC; terminal notifies the RCC directly.
Both facility types notify only the ARTCC, which determines whether RCC notification is warranted.

items.explanation: Paragraph 10-2-10a establishes en route facilities notify the RCC directly. Paragraph 10-2-10b establishes terminal facilities notify the ARTCC, which coordinates with the RCC. Terminal facilities never contact the RCC directly for ELT events.

items.instruction: Identify when use of the EOVM or EAM is authorized.
items.question_text: Under which condition may the EOVM or EAM be used to provide emergency advisory service to an aircraft?

choices.text: The pilot has declared an emergency, or the controller has determined an emergency condition exists or is imminent.
The controller wants to provide a more direct route that would descend an aircraft below the MVA during routine operations.
The pilot asks for a demonstration of the EOVM capabilities during a routine VFR training flight.
The controller needs to vector an aircraft around weather and the shortest available path goes below the published minimum altitude.

items.explanation: Paragraph 10-2-17a states the EOVM/EAM must only be used under two conditions: the pilot has declared an emergency, or the controller has determined that an emergency condition exists or is imminent because the pilot cannot maintain appropriate terrain/obstacle clearance. Routine operations do not qualify.

items.instruction: Identify which information item is added when the ARTCC forwards an overdue aircraft report to the RCC.
items.question_text: When the ARTCC forwards information about an overdue aircraft to the RCC, which item is added that was not on the terminal facility's notification to the ARTCC?

choices.text: Action taken by the reporting facility and proposed action.
Aircraft beacon code.
Flight plan including color of aircraft.
Last position report and how determined.

items.explanation: The ARTCC adds 'facility and person calling' and 'action taken by reporting facility and proposed action' when forwarding to the RCC. These two items are not on the terminal-to-ARTCC list in paragraph 10-3-2. Items like beacon code, flight plan, and position report appear on both lists.

items.instruction: Identify the correct time reference for the 30-minute traffic restriction.
items.question_text: An overdue IFR aircraft was both given an approach clearance at 1415Z and assigned an EFC time of 1430Z. Which time reference starts the 30-minute IFR traffic restriction clock?

choices.text: 1415Z — the approach clearance time takes precedence over the EFC time.
1430Z — the EFC time is the mandatory reference for all lost-communication time calculations.
1445Z — the restriction clock begins 30 minutes after the earlier of the two references.
The time the flight plan was filed — it is the only time reference common to all overdue aircraft situations.

items.explanation: Paragraph 10-4-1 lists five time references in priority order. The approach clearance time (subpara a) has higher priority than EFC time (subpara b). When multiple references apply, use the highest-priority one that is available.

items.instruction: Determine the effect of withdrawing concurrence after the traffic suspension period expires.
items.question_text: After the 30-minute suspension period for an overdue aircraft expires, all affected pilots concur to resume normal ATC. Fifteen minutes later, one pilot withdraws that concurrence. What happens to normal ATC operations?

choices.text: Normal ATC must be suspended again because concurrence must be maintained for a full 30 minutes after the suspension period expires.
Normal ATC continues because concurrence was valid at the moment the suspension period originally expired.
Normal ATC continues for all aircraft except the one whose pilot withdrew concurrence.
The controller must restart the 30-minute suspension period from the time concurrence was withdrawn.

items.explanation: Paragraph 10-4-3 requires that concurrence be maintained for a period of 30 minutes after the suspension period expires. If concurrence is withdrawn during that 30-minute window, normal ATC must be suspended again.

items.instruction: Identify which ATC action is permitted at an airport underlying an active Debris Response Area.
items.question_text: A DRA has been activated and an airport lies underneath it. Which action may ATC take at that airport?

choices.text: Continue broadcasting the mishap alert to affected aircraft on all appropriate frequencies.
Issue a takeoff clearance to an aircraft holding short of the runway.
Issue a landing clearance to an arriving aircraft on final approach.
Issue taxi instructions for an aircraft to reposition from the ramp to the active runway.

items.explanation: Paragraph 10-5-2 prohibits ATC from issuing takeoff or landing clearances at airports underlying an active DRA. However, the alert broadcast obligation continues — it must be reissued every 5 minutes for 20 minutes following the last known event.

items.instruction: Identify the correct ordering of oceanic emergency phases.
items.question_text: Which sequence correctly orders the three oceanic emergency phases from least to most severe?

choices.text: INCERFA (Uncertainty), ALERFA (Alert), DETRESFA (Distress).
ALERFA (Alert), INCERFA (Uncertainty), DETRESFA (Distress).
DETRESFA (Distress), ALERFA (Alert), INCERFA (Uncertainty).
INCERFA (Uncertainty), DETRESFA (Distress), ALERFA (Alert).

items.explanation: Paragraph 10-6-2 describes three escalating emergency phases. INCERFA (Uncertainty) is the least severe, triggered by loss of communication for 30+ minutes. ALERFA (Alert) is intermediate, triggered when communication attempts fail. DETRESFA (Distress) is most severe, triggered when fuel is exhausted or forced landing is imminent.

items.instruction: Identify the correct conditions for discontinuing IFR separation between SAR and distressed aircraft.
items.question_text: A SAR aircraft has established visual contact with a distressed aircraft in oceanic airspace. The SAR pilot concurs to discontinue IFR separation, but the distressed pilot has not yet responded. May IFR separation be discontinued?

choices.text: No — both pilots must concur in addition to visual or radar contact being established by the SAR aircraft.
Yes — the SAR pilot's concurrence alone is sufficient once visual contact has been established.
Yes — visual contact by the SAR aircraft automatically terminates the IFR separation requirement.
No — IFR separation may never be discontinued between SAR aircraft and aircraft in distress in oceanic airspace.

items.explanation: Paragraph 10-6-5a requires BOTH visual or radar contact by the SAR aircraft AND concurrence of pilots of BOTH aircraft before IFR separation may be discontinued.

items.instruction: Identify the correct IFR/SVFR lateral separation minimum for a ground missile emergency.
items.question_text: When rerouting an IFR aircraft under radar control around a ground missile emergency location that is accurately depicted on radar, what lateral separation minimum applies?

choices.text: 1 mile between the emergency location and the aircraft.
3 miles, which is the standard terminal radar separation minimum.
5 miles, which is the standard en route radar separation minimum.
2 miles, which is the reduced separation standard authorized for emergency reroutes.

items.explanation: Paragraph 10-7-2a specifies 1 mile lateral separation between the emergency location and an aircraft under radar control when the location can be accurately determined by reference to the radar scope.

items.front: How do Distress and Urgency conditions differ?
items.back: Distress: serious and/or imminent danger requiring immediate assistance. Urgency: concerning safety of an aircraft, vehicle, or person but not requiring immediate assistance.

items.front: What external facilities may a controller enlist for an aircraft in distress, and when?
items.back: Available radar facilities operated by FAA, military, and FCC, and their emergency services. Enlist them when the pilot requests or when the controller deems necessary.

items.front: What three categories of aircraft trigger the controller's coordination obligation?
items.back: Aircraft believed to be overdue, lost, or in emergency status. Coordinate efforts to the extent possible for all three categories.

items.front: What hazards can nearby aircraft create for emergency rescue operations?
items.back: Congestion, distraction, and wake turbulence from nearby airplanes and helicopters. These can prevent or delay proper execution of rescue or relief operations.

items.front: When may a controller request supplementary emergency information from a low-altitude military fighter-type aircraft?
items.back: Request the aircraft's position if not visually sighted, on radar, or given by the pilot. All other supplementary items should normally not be requested from low-altitude military fighter-type aircraft.

items.front: Why is climb particularly dangerous for an aircraft inside a volcanic ash cloud?
items.back: Engine damage from ash ingestion increases as engine power increases. High-bypass turbofan engines (e.g., B747) are especially vulnerable. Climb while in the ash cloud is avoided where terrain permits.

items.front: What three steps must a controller take when a VFR aircraft in weather difficulty needs a frequency change?
items.back: 1. Advise the pilot of the reason. 2. Request contact with the appropriate facility. 3. Inform that facility of the situation. If the aircraft cannot communicate with the new facility, relay information and clearances.

items.front: What three paths allow radar assistance to a VFR pilot who encounters IFR weather?
items.back: 1. Pilot is IFR qualified/capable — file IFR and issue clearance. 2. Pilot not qualified but emergency exists — provide assistance. 3. Pilot refuses to declare emergency but controller determines emergency conditions exist — may still assist.

items.front: What is the difference between the Terminal Area EOVM and the En Route EAM?
items.back: EOVM: terminal obstruction video map. EAM: en route emergency altitude map. Both facilitate advisory service when terrain/obstacle clearance minimum altitude cannot be maintained.

items.front: What two items does the ARTCC add when forwarding an overdue aircraft report to the RCC?
items.back: 1. Facility and person calling. 2. Action taken by reporting facility and proposed action. These items are not on the terminal-to-ARTCC list.

items.front: What five time references start the 30-minute traffic restriction period for an overdue aircraft?
items.back: In priority order: 1. Approach clearance delivery time. 2. EFC time. 3. NAVAID arrival estimate. 4. Controller's current estimate. 5. Flight plan filing time (last resort).

items.front: What two requirements govern resuming normal ATC after a traffic suspension period expires?
items.back: 1. Affected operators or pilots must concur. 2. Concurrence must be maintained for 30 minutes after the suspension period expires. Withdrawal during that window suspends operations again.

items.front: When must an IFR lost-communications situation also be treated as suspicious activity?
items.back: When communications have not been reestablished after 5 minutes. Consider the aircraft's or pilot's activity suspicious and report it to the OS/CIC while continuing appropriate lost-communications actions.

items.front: How often must the debris mishap alert broadcast be reissued after a space launch or reentry vehicle mishap?
items.back: Every 5 minutes for 20 minutes following the last known event, or until the DRA is deactivated.

items.front: Where are the oceanic emergency procedures in Chapter 10 Section 6 applied?
items.back: Solely in oceanic airspace. They do not apply to domestic en route, terminal, or ground operations.

items.front: What are the three oceanic emergency phases and their primary triggers?
items.back: INCERFA: no communication within 30 min of expected time. ALERFA: subsequent comm attempts fail or aircraft fails to arrive. DETRESFA: fuel exhausted or forced landing imminent.

items.front: How do en route and terminal notification paths differ for a ground missile emergency?
items.back: En route: alerting advisories by air carrier company radio (VFR company aircraft) and FSSs adjacent to emergency. Terminal: relay all information to ARTCC and disseminate locally.

items.front: What visible hazard must all aircraft be advised to avoid near a ground missile emergency?
items.back: Any observed smoke columns. Advise all aircraft to avoid them, regardless of flight rules.

items.front: What notification is available for an extended-duration ground missile emergency, and which facility type issues it?
items.back: A Notice to Airmen (NOTAM) may be issued. This is an en route facility responsibility.
